theory.intro: Mass tells us how heavy something is. We choose a unit and a measuring device to suit the size of the object.
facts: **Grams ($\text{g}$)** suit light objects (an apple, a pencil).
**Kilograms ($\text{kg}$)** suit everyday objects (a bag of flour, a person).
**Tonnes ($\text{t}$)** suit very heavy objects (a car, a truck, an elephant).
Kitchen scales measure grams; bathroom scales measure kilograms; a weighbridge measures tonnes.
steps: Think about how heavy the object is.
Pick $\text{g}$ for light, $\text{kg}$ for medium, $\text{t}$ for very heavy.
Choose a device that can read that unit.

foundation.q: Choose the best unit to measure the mass of a $\textbf{strawberry}$: $\text{g}$, $\text{kg}$ or $\text{t}$.
Choose the best unit to measure the mass of a $\textbf{watermelon}$: $\text{g}$, $\text{kg}$ or $\text{t}$.
Choose the best unit to measure the mass of a $\textbf{truck}$: $\text{g}$, $\text{kg}$ or $\text{t}$.
Choose the best unit to measure the mass of a $\textbf{pencil}$: $\text{g}$, $\text{kg}$ or $\text{t}$.
Choose the best unit to measure the mass of a $\textbf{bag of cement}$: $\text{g}$, $\text{kg}$ or $\text{t}$.
Choose the best unit to measure the mass of an $\textbf{elephant}$: $\text{g}$, $\text{kg}$ or $\text{t}$.
Which device best measures the mass of $\textbf{flour for a cake}$: kitchen scales, bathroom scales or a weighbridge?
Which device best measures the mass of a $\textbf{person}$: kitchen scales, bathroom scales or a weighbridge?
Which device best measures the mass of a $\textbf{loaded truck}$: kitchen scales, bathroom scales or a weighbridge?
Choose the best unit to measure the mass of a $\textbf{single grape}$: $\text{g}$, $\text{kg}$ or $\text{t}$.

development.q: A bag of apples has mass about $2$ ___. Fill in $\text{g}$, $\text{kg}$ or $\text{t}$.

solution.math: $2\text{ g is too light, } 2\text{ t too heavy}$

development.q: A small car has mass about $1$ ___. Fill in $\text{g}$, $\text{kg}$ or $\text{t}$.

solution.math: $1\text{ kg is far too light for a car}$

development.q: A slice of bread has mass about $30$ ___. Fill in $\text{g}$, $\text{kg}$ or $\text{t}$.
Which is the better estimate for a newborn baby: $3\text{ g}$, $3\text{ kg}$ or $3\text{ t}$?

solution.math: $3\text{ g is a feather, } 3\text{ t a truck}$

development.q: Which is the better estimate for a $20$-cent coin: $11\text{ g}$, $11\text{ kg}$ or $11\text{ t}$?
Which is the better estimate for a fully grown horse: $500\text{ g}$, $500\text{ kg}$ or $500\text{ t}$?

solution.math: $500\text{ g is too light, } 500\text{ t absurd}$

development.q: Name a unit and a device you would use to weigh sugar for a recipe.
Name a unit and a device you would use to weigh a delivery of sand on a truck.
A dictionary, a feather and a bus need weighing. Match each to $\text{g}$, $\text{kg}$ or $\text{t}$.
Would you use $\text{g}$, $\text{kg}$ or $\text{t}$ to state the mass carried by a cargo ship?

mastery.q: A recipe needs $250\text{ g}$ of butter. Which device should you use, and why not bathroom scales?
mastery.a: kitchen scales; bathroom scales are too coarse

mastery.q: Sam says his school bag is $4\text{ t}$. Explain why this is wrong and give a better unit.
mastery.a: should be $\text{kg}$ — $4\text{ t}$ is the mass of a small truck

solution.note: Far too heavy for a bag

mastery.q: A jeweller weighs a gold ring. Suggest a unit and explain why grams suit better than kilograms.
mastery.a: grams — a ring is only a few grams

mastery.q: Order these from lightest to heaviest by likely unit: paper clip, a dog, a shipping container.
A farmer weighs both a single egg and a full crate of eggs. Suggest a unit for each.
Why would a weighbridge be unsuitable for measuring a $30\text{ g}$ spice sample?
mastery.a: it reads in tonnes, far too coarse for $30\text{ g}$

mastery.q: A vet needs to weigh a kitten precisely. Recommend a unit and device.
A bridge sign reads 'limit $10$ ___'. Which unit makes sense and why?
List, with a reason, the best unit for: a postage letter, a microwave oven, a whale.
A shop sells loose lollies. Which unit and device let the shopkeeper charge fairly by mass?
